Q1: My study routine was honestly pretty minimal in terms of hours, maybe around seven hours a week, but I made every minute count. Being 29 and juggling other responsibilities, I had to be strategic about when I studied. I'm a visual learner, so I'd create color-coded summaries and diagrams rather than spending hours re-reading. I usually carved out time on weekends and maybe one or two evenings, though it wasn't always consistent.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied depending on what was happening in my life and when deadlines were approaching. Some weeks I'd be more consistent, but honestly, when work got busy or anxiety spiked, my schedule would shift dramatically. I found myself cramming more toward deadlines rather than maintaining a steady rhythm throughout the semester. The unpredictability of my other commitments made it hard to stick to a fixed schedule.
Q3: Usually I logged in when I knew an assignment was coming up or when I needed to check announcements. Sometimes my peer support group would mention something in our chat, and that would prompt me to log in and see what I'd missed. I wasn't someone who checked the LMS daily out of habit—it was more purposeful, driven by specific needs. Occasionally anxiety would push me to check if I'd missed something important.
Q4: Honestly, the LMS never really became a natural part of my routine the way I think it does for some students. It felt more like a tool I used when necessary rather than a habit. The peer support connections helped somewhat—knowing others were active made me want to stay somewhat engaged. But with limited resources and time, I prioritized efficiency over frequent check-ins.
Q5: The video lectures were incredibly useful for me because I'm such a visual learner. I could pause, rewind, and really absorb the diagrams and demonstrations at my own pace. The discussion forums were also valuable when my peer support group was active—seeing different perspectives helped clarify concepts. Anything with visual components or that allowed me to connect with peers felt worth my time.
Q6: I often skipped the lengthy reading materials or supplementary PDFs because I just didn't have time to get through them all. Some of the optional quizzes I ignored because they felt like busywork rather than genuine learning tools. With limited resources and high anxiety, I had to triage what I engaged with, and text-heavy resources without visual elements got pushed aside.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to exam content or helped me understand a concept I was struggling with. Visual resources, peer discussions, and anything that gave me feedback on my understanding felt valuable. If I couldn't see how something would help my grade or actual comprehension, my motivation to engage dropped significantly given everything else on my plate.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't always afford to engage with, given my time constraints and stress levels. Required activities had clear stakes, so even when my motivation was low, I pushed through. Optional ones required me to convince myself they were worth the effort, and with high anxiety, that extra mental hurdle often meant I skipped them. There was also less guilt associated with ignoring optional work.
Q9: Absolutely—I downloaded lecture slides and videos early in the semester and studied from those offline constantly. I'd print diagrams and create visual study sheets that I'd review during my commute or lunch breaks. I also used external YouTube videos to supplement concepts I didn't fully grasp from course materials. A lot of my actual learning happened away from the LMS entirely.
Q10: Most of my exam preparation happened offline with printed materials and handwritten notes spread across my kitchen table. I'd spend hours reviewing my visual summaries and diagrams without touching the LMS at all. My peer support group also met in person sometimes, and those conversations were incredibly valuable for my understanding but left no digital trace. The LMS really only captured a fraction of my study time.
Q11: Yes, this was my standard approach. I'd download everything I needed in one session, then work through it over days or weeks without logging back in. With inconsistent internet access at home sometimes and limited data on my phone, downloading once and studying offline was more practical. My exam score reflects learning that happened mostly outside the platform.
Q12: Mid-semester was particularly rough when my anxiety peaked and multiple deadlines collided. There were weeks when just opening my laptop felt overwhelming, and I'd avoid the LMS because seeing all the pending tasks made my stress worse. Financial pressures and work demands also competed for my mental energy, making it harder to engage even when I wanted to. The peer support helped pull me back when I was drifting.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted from work or overwhelmed by competing responsibilities, my LMS usage dropped to almost nothing. Confusion about certain topics sometimes made me avoid those modules rather than face them directly. Fatigue meant I'd log in, stare at the screen, and log out without accomplishing much. My engagement was heavily dependent on my mental and emotional state on any given day.
Q14: There were times I'd watch videos or scroll through materials while my mind was completely elsewhere, consumed by worry or exhaustion. I'd technically be 'engaging' with content but retaining almost nothing. Sometimes I'd open discussion posts and read them without really processing the information. Those sessions felt hollow—going through the motions without genuine learning happening.
Q15: I postponed assignments more than I'd like to admit, even knowing they were important. My anxiety made starting tasks feel monumental, so I'd delay until the pressure of the deadline forced action. Some discussion posts I avoided because formulating responses felt draining when I was already stressed. The irony is that postponing usually made my anxiety worse, but in the moment, avoidance felt like self-preservation.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my primary motivator for LMS engagement. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly become very active—downloading materials, watching videos, participating in discussions. Without that external pressure, my motivation wasn't strong enough to drive consistent engagement. My usage patterns probably looked very spiky, with bursts of activity followed by quiet periods.
Q17: Feedback definitely affected my engagement, though not always positively. Good feedback motivated me to keep going and reassured me I was on the right track. But when feedback was critical or I received a lower score than expected, my anxiety would spike and I'd sometimes avoid the course for a few days. The peer support helped me process feedback more constructively than I would have alone.
Q18: The course structure with clear deadlines and organized modules helped me know what to prioritize, which I appreciated given my limited time. However, the volume of content sometimes felt overwhelming, and I wished there were clearer indicators of what was essential versus supplementary. The peer support intervention was genuinely helpful—having that connection made the course feel less isolating during stressful periods.
Q19: Visual materials and well-organized modules made engagement easier for me. What made it harder was when there were too many text-heavy resources without clear visual summaries, or when navigation felt confusing. The discussion forums were helpful when active but felt discouraging when posts went unanswered. Having peer support built into the course structure was probably the single most helpful design element for someone like me.
Q20: I think that could reflect anxiety or feeling lost—clicking around trying to understand what's expected without successfully completing work. It might also be students who are genuinely trying but struggling with the material or with time management. Sometimes high activity is actually a sign of confusion rather than engagement. Or they might be dealing with personal challenges that make completion difficult despite effort.
Q21: This absolutely reflects my experience. My exam score was high despite relatively low LMS activity because I studied extensively offline with downloaded materials and visual notes. The LMS captured maybe a quarter of my actual study effort. Some of us learn effectively through methods that don't leave digital footprints, and that doesn't mean we're not engaged—just that our engagement looks different.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show login patterns, deadline-driven behavior, and which resources get clicked. But they completely miss offline studying, the quality of engagement during logged sessions, and the emotional experience of learning. They can't capture the anxiety I felt opening certain modules or the relief when peer conversations clarified confusing concepts. Logs show behavior, not understanding or effort.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely grappling with the material and caring about understanding it, not just completing tasks. For me, it's when I'm actively connecting new concepts to what I already know, creating visual representations, and discussing ideas with peers. It's a mental state of curiosity and investment, which doesn't always correlate with time spent on a platform. True engagement often happens in my head, away from any trackable system.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much my peer support connections meant to me emotionally, or how they helped me push through when anxiety made me want to give up. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent with printed diagrams, the conversations over coffee with study partners, or the mental effort of managing high anxiety while still performing well. My distinction grade came from resilience and strategic studying that happened largely invisible to any tracking system.
